briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. I will now begin the climate change expert spokesperson briefing for July 18, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Unprecedented Climate Disasters and Health Threats
sections.content: On the Korean Peninsula, extreme rainfall events caused by climate change, such as 10% of a year's worth of rain falling in just one hour, are reshaping flood maps [2]. Globally, 670,000 people die annually from wildfire smoke, a serious issue that attacks all bodily organs, disrupts sleep, and threatens health [4, 18, 20, 35]. Particularly in the United States and Canada, wildfires are increasing, and in Arizona, wildfires are leading to the worst floods, indicating that climate change is fueling complex disasters [28, 32]. Furthermore, climate change is depleting nutrients in food, increasing the risk of waterborne diseases, and causing ecological disturbances such as the spread of migratory pests from China [5, 17, 24].

sections.title: 🌍 Threatened Human Cultural Heritage and Ecosystems
sections.content: Climate change poses a direct threat to humanity's precious cultural heritage. Domestically, Heo Min, head of the National Heritage Administration, emphasized the need to continuously explore preservation measures for the Bangudae Petroglyphs by installing sluice gates at Sayeon Dam by 2030 [1, 16]. Internationally, the UN and UNESCO are set to designate additional world heritage sites 'in danger' due to conflict and climate change, and will review their inclusion on the list of sites in crisis [23, 25, 27, 30]. Meanwhile, a 37-year-long forest floor heating experiment revealed that carbon trapped in the soil has begun to be released, suggesting that climate change can affect natural carbon reservoirs in unexpected ways [22]. These changes are causing complex societal issues, including investigations into the link between climate change and statelessness [26].

sections.title: 🌱 Expansion of Renewable Energy and Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Amidst these crises, global efforts and positive progress continue for climate change response and carbon neutrality. Global renewable energy generation capacity has reached 5.2 TW, reshaping market structures [14, 15], and China has completed the world's highest-head pumped-storage power plant, expected to serve as a super-large battery for the renewable energy era [9]. Domestically, the Suseong-gu mayor hosted a climate change response forum with a German delegation [3], and Goyang City and Yeongwol County are making local-level efforts through the appointment of carbon neutrality citizen participation groups and strengthening climate crisis response capabilities [6, 7]. Companies like Orion are also advancing ESG management, including carbon neutrality, to a global standard [8], and Garden Project has showcased innovative attempts to combine renewable energy with rainwater circulation systems [13]. Furthermore, a city in Arizona, USA, is demonstrating a best practice in reducing heat-related deaths, presenting the possibility of climate adaptation [33].

sections.title: ⚠️ Challenges and Realistic Limitations of Climate Policy
sections.content: However, policy challenges and realistic limitations in achieving climate goals are also clearly emerging. Domestically, warnings have been raised that some mega-projects could become carbon 'black holes,' along with the necessity of adjusting the pace according to renewable energy principles [10]. The former director of the Korea Power Exchange pointed out that achieving 100 GW of renewable energy by 2030 is unlikely, and it will be difficult to respond to the AI era without electricity tariff reform, indicating challenges in domestic energy policy [11]. Internationally, the European Union (EU) has proposed measures to slow down the pace of corporate carbon emission reductions, showing a move towards seeking flexibility in achieving climate goals [34]. Filmmaker Josh Fox commented on the 'unfolding catastrophe of climate change,' reflecting the general recognition that creative assistance is needed to understand and reverse human-induced climate change [21, 29]. Experts in the Tampa Bay area are discussing how climate change is reshaping the region, demonstrating the urgent need for tailored responses to local realities [19].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the accelerating unprecedented disasters and threats caused by climate change. Simultaneously, while global efforts for carbon neutrality and the expansion of renewable energy continue, in-depth discussions and practical actions are even more urgently needed to overcome policy challenges and realistic limitations. This concludes the daily climate briefing for July 18, 2026.

articles.description: Heo Min, Administrator of the National Heritage Administration, is greeting participants at the 8th World Heritage Site Managers Forum held at Ulsan Exhibition and Convention Center on the 17th. (Provided by National Heritage Administration) 'We plan to start the construction of the Sayeon Dam floodgates in the second half of this year and complete it by 2030 to preserve the Bangudae Petroglyphs. However, as climate change becomes increasingly severe, we must also consider whether the floodgates will be able to fulfill their role even after completion...'

summary.phenomenon: Heo Min, Administrator of the National Heritage Administration, announced at the 8th World Heritage Site Managers Forum held at Ulsan Exhibition and Convention Center on the 17th that construction of the Sayeon Dam floodgates to preserve the Bangudae Petroglyphs will begin in the second half of this year and be completed by 2030.
summary.cause: Concerns about the preservation of the Bangudae Petroglyphs are growing as climate change becomes increasingly severe.
summary.outlook: It was mentioned that continuous consideration is needed regarding whether the floodgates will be able to fulfill their role amidst climate change even after their completion by 2030.

articles.title: ‘It’s only going to get worse’: wildfires forcing firefighters to make impossible choices
articles.description: As the climate crisis fuels more intense blazes, pushing them to new parts of the world, those tackling them are forced to ration resources and decide which to fightCésar Alcaraz had only just become a firefighter in the late 1990s when he found himself ambushed by a fast-moving blaze. Barely able to breathe and with no more water left in his truck, he and his colleagues fled an inferno ravaging Spain’s Montgó mountain region, wishing their bosses had sent more support.But nearly three decades o

summary.phenomenon: In the late 1990s, firefighters in Spain's Montgó mountain region faced rapidly spreading wildfires and struggled with resource shortages. Globally, the climate crisis is fueling more intense blazes, which are spreading to new parts of the world.
summary.cause: The climate crisis is fueling more intense wildfires, leading to their spread into new regions globally.
summary.outlook: As the climate crisis intensifies, wildfires are expected to worsen, and firefighters will face difficult choices in rationing resources and deciding which fires to combat.
